title: Demo Resume - Systems I Have Built

scenes.title: Opening
scenes.editIntent: Title card for the demo resume. Establish that this is a system-level portfolio, not a static list of jobs. The scene should feel cinematic and deliberate.

blocks.text: This is not a resume.
It is a set of working systems.
Each role becomes a demo.

scenes.title: Career System Map
scenes.editIntent: Career overview as a systems map. Show the progression from embedded diagnostics to enterprise platforms, financial automation, and AI orchestration. The point is architectural continuity, not chronology.

blocks.text: These were never isolated jobs.
They were connected systems problems.
The pattern is architecture, automation, and operational clarity.

scenes.title: Edward Jones - Problem
scenes.editIntent: Frame the Edward Jones platform problem state before the redesign. Emphasize monolithic coupling, cross-team friction, and the scale of delivery coordination across thirteen agile teams.

blocks.text: Edward Jones had a delivery problem, not just a code problem.
Thirteen teams were tied to one tightly coupled platform.
Release friction was structural.

scenes.title: Edward Jones - Transformation
scenes.editIntent: Show the target architecture and capability shift at Edward Jones. Micro frontends, service decomposition, AWS, and continuous delivery should read as an operating model change, not just a diagram.

blocks.text: The answer was architectural separation.
Micro frontends, service boundaries, and cloud delivery changed the team topology.
Coordination gave way to autonomy.

scenes.title: Platform Command Center
scenes.editIntent: Translate the Edward Jones modernization story into a live command-center frame. The system should look operable: services, events, AI terminal, and guided action.

blocks.text: Once the platform is decomposed, you can operate it.
You can see incidents form, isolate blast radius, and act with confidence.
That is the difference between a platform and a monolith.

scenes.title: Edward Jones - Impact
scenes.editIntent: Summarize the organizational impact of the Edward Jones modernization. Quantify the release-time reduction and show how architecture removed friction across teams.

blocks.text: Release time dropped by fifty percent.
Governance became systematic instead of reactive.
Architecture removed friction from thirteen teams at once.

scenes.title: BPM - Financial Automation
scenes.editIntent: Represent BPM Software Solutions as an automation and financial operations system. Show ingestion, normalization, automation, AI, and reporting as one continuous flow grounded in the experience data.

blocks.text: At BPM, the work was turning messy operational inputs into reliable business systems.
ETL, integrations, AI, and automation all sat in the same pipeline.
The result was less manual overhead and faster decisions.

scenes.title: RenderX - AI Orchestration
scenes.editIntent: Show RenderX as an orchestration system: plugin model, event flow, and the insight that too much structure can become its own failure mode.

blocks.text: RenderX was an experiment in orchestrated intelligence.
Events, plugins, and structured outputs made the system extensible.
It also taught a harder lesson: complexity can become the product.

scenes.title: Snap-On - Embedded Diagnostics
scenes.editIntent: Represent the Snap-On work as the systems foundation: diagnostic tooling, adaptive protocols, and communication across heterogeneous vehicle networks.

blocks.text: This foundation started in embedded diagnostics.
Vehicle protocols, adaptive communication, and real-time troubleshooting shaped the systems mindset early.
One system, many communication worlds.

scenes.title: What This Resume Is
scenes.editIntent: Final meta scene. Close the argument that the portfolio is a set of runnable proofs rather than a static document. Land the language cleanly and directly.

blocks.text: This is not what I say I built.
It is how the systems actually work.
That is the resume.
